terms.definition: Degree to which parts of a system hold together in stable, mutually intelligible relationship
Externally supplied coherence-restoring energy, usually via voluntary constraint adoption
Rate at which disorder outpaces existing coherence structures
Value of coherence (χ) below which feedback loops cannot restore systemic order
Entropy in an isolated system never decreases over time
Quantitative measure of disorder or unavailable energy in a system
System that exchanges energy or matter with its environment, allowing local entropy reduction
System with no energy or matter exchange; entropy strictly increases
Path-dependence in state transitions; reversal requires overshoot beyond original critical point
Abrupt systemic change when control parameters cross a critical threshold
Words that both describe and evaluate actions, embedding norms within semantics (e.g., 'courage', 'duty')
Small group whose practices generate disproportionate civilizational influence
Freely chosen self-limitation that increases order and meaning
Loss of shared stable reference frames necessary for low-cost coordination
Statistical dispersion of family stability indicators such as marriage persistence and intergenerational cohesion
